elements.sent-2411-003: Eisl\"offel\inst{\ref{inst1 \institute{Th\"uringer Landessternwarte Tautenburg, Sternwarte 5, D-07778 Tautenburg, Germany email{bfuhrmeister@tls-tautenburg.de \and Hamburger Sternwarte, Universit\"at Hamburg, Gojenbergsweg 112, 21029 Hamburg, Germany \and Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics, University of Colorado Boulder, Boulder, CO 80303, USA \and European Southern Observatory, Karl-Schwarzschild-Strasse 2, 85748 Garching bei M\"unchen, Germany \abstract {Understanding the interstellar and potentially circumstellar extinction in the sight lines of classical T Tauri stars is an important ingredient for constructing reliable spectral energy distributions, which catalyze protoplanetary disk chemistry, for example.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Therefore, some attempts of measuring $A_{V}$ toward individual stars have been made using partly different wavelength regimes and different underlying assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We used strong lines of Ly$\alpha$ fluorescent \mbox{H$_{2}$} and derived the extinction based on the assumption of optically thin transitions.
elements.sent-2411-006: We investigated a sample of 72 classical T Tauri stars observed with the Hubble Space Telescope in the framework of the ULLYSES program.
elements.sent-2411-007: We computed $A_V$ and $R_V$ values for the 34 objects with sufficient data quality and an additionally $A_V$ value for the canonical $R_V=3.1$ value.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our results agree largely with values obtained from optical data.
elements.sent-2411-009: Moreover, we confirm the degeneracy between $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ and present possibilities to break this.
elements.sent-2411-010: Finally, we discuss whether the assumption of optical thin lines is valid.
elements.sent-2411-011: Reddening laws predict the visual extinction $A_{V}$=E(B-V)$\cdot$ $R_{V}$ depending on the wavelength, here, the Johnson V and B band.
elements.sent-2411-012: Reddening laws use some measure of the total dust column E(B-V) \citep{Predehl1995 and a parameter to characterize the grain distribution $R_{V}$ with a canonical value of $R_{V}$=3.1 for the interstellar medium that is mainly derived from optical and IR data \citep{Schultz1975, Rieke1985 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Extinction curves show an increase toward the UV, whose slope is characterized by the size of the grains causing the extinction.
elements.sent-2411-014: Large grains produce flatter curves, that is, closer to wavelength independent or gray extinction.
elements.sent-2411-015: These curves are characterized by $R_{V}$ values well above 3.1, and measurements up to about $R\sim5.6$ have been reported for dense molecular clouds \citep{Cardelli1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Rayleigh scattering would produce steep distributions with $R_{V}$=1.2 (while the steepest measurement yields about $R_{V}$=2.1; see \citet{Welty1992 and the review of \citet{Draine2003 for further details).}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Higher extinction values $A_{V}$ and steeper distributions $R_{V}$ lead to a more pronounced reddening, that is, a greater loss at bluer wavelengths, especially in the UV, and they therefore alter the spectral energy distribution (SED) of an object, by which the UV part is most strongly affected.
elements.sent-2411-018: Knowledge of the absolute UV radiation is crucial in the context of young cool stars, namely classical T Tauri stars (CTTSs).
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, the UV excess is commonly used to derive mass-accretion rates, and it is therefore crucial for the development of protoplanetary disks (see, e.g., \citet{Schindhelm2012 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-020: The protoplanetary disks themselves may contribute to the extinction in addition to the ISM when the line of sight passes through them.
elements.sent-2411-021: The gas-to-dust ratio of parts of the protoplanetary disks can be similar to that of the ISM \citep{Schneider2015 , but it may also be much lower \citep{Schneider2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the latter case, extinction is caused by regions with different grain sizes, and individual extinction values are needed for each star.
elements.sent-2411-023: A thorough discussion of the different components in the line of sight for CTTSs was given by \citet{McJunkin2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: We estimate the $A_{V}$ values using the far-UV (FUV; $\sim$912-1700\,\AA) spectrum of the star itself using Hubble space telescope (HST) data covering 1150-1650\,\AA.
elements.sent-2411-025: We specifically used the strongest fluorescent lines of \mbox{H$_{2}$} pumped by Ly$\alpha$ flux incident on the warm disk.
elements.sent-2411-026: Cold \mbox{H$_{2}$} ($\approx$ 10\,K) does not radiate effectively because it has no permanent dipole \citep{Sternberg1988 , and therefore, the observed quadrupole ro-vibrational transitions in the IR are typically weak.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, dipole-allowed electronic transitions exist in the FUV and result in strong \mbox{H$_{2}$} emission lines.
elements.sent-2411-028: These are fluorescent lines that are mainly photo-excited (pumped) by Ly$\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-029: While these lines were first observed for the Sun by \citet{Jordan1977 , the idea of Ly$\alpha$ pumping was developed by \citet{Shull1978 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: More than 100 of these lines have been found in CTTSs \citep{Herczeg2002, Herczeg2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: They may originate from more than ten upper levels in the $B^{1}\Sigma^{+ _{u}$ electronic state \citep{Herczeg2006 .}}
elements.sent-2411-032:
Since the Einstein coefficients for spontaneous emissions are large for these levels of \mbox{H$_{2}$} \citep[A$_{ul}\approx10^{8}$\,s$^{-1}$, see ][]{Abgrall1993 , they immediately decay into diverse lower levels in the ground state $X^{1}\Sigma^{+ _{g}$, depending on the branching ratios B$_{ij}$=A$_{ij}$/$\sum{A_{[
u', J'] }$, where [$
u$', J'] is called a progression and is defined by all transitions from the upper level [$
u$', J'] (with $
u$' the vibrational quantum number and J' the rotational quantum number) to different $
u$'' and J'' in the ground state.}}}

elements.sent-2411-034: These FUV \mbox{H$_{2}$} lines were used for $A_{V}$ measurements by \citet{McJunkin2016 , who included radiative transport simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Some radiative transport effects are expected because the same material that absorbs the pumping photons, mostly stellar Ly$\alpha$ photons, also absorbs part of the then fluorescently emitted H$_2$ photons.
elements.sent-2411-036: This is particularly important because this self-absorption affects the flux ratios of different H$_2$ lines in a qualitatively similar pattern as absorption by dust grains, that is, the observed flux at shorter wavelength is reduced.
elements.sent-2411-037: Therefore, there is some degeneracy in the $A_V$ estimate and in the optical depth effect, which may be addressed using systems with low $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-038: We attempted to use H$_2$ line flux ratios without any radiative transport calculations, that is, we assumed that the lines are optically thin.
elements.sent-2411-039: This simplified the approach, and we did not need to specify an a priori unknown geometry and density structure.
elements.sent-2411-040: Nevertheless, we are aware that not all \mbox{H$_{2}$} lines may be treated under the assumption that they are optically thin.
elements.sent-2411-041: Our rationale therefore was the following: When we compare the results of progressions that include lines at a short wavelength that may be hampered by self-absorption with the results from progressions that only encompass lines at a longer wavelength where self-absorption becomes negligible, we may be able to identify possible contributions of self-absorption, especially when we also use stars that are known to have low $A_{V}$ values.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our paper is structured as follows: In Sect. \ref{sec:obs we briefly describe the archival data we use and the line flux measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Our novel method for obtaining $A_{V}$ is explained in Sect. \ref{sec:method .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We present our results in Sect.~\ref{sec:results , and we discuss the difficulties of the method in Sect. \ref{sec:discussion .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The FUV spectra analyzed here were all part of the data release of the Ultraviolet Legacy Library of Young Stars as Essential Standards (ULLYSES) program \citep{Ullyses2020 , which dedicated 487 HST orbits to low-mass stars, but also included older data.}

elements.sent-2411-053: Since the applied threshold was chosen only to filter out the worst cases, we reduced the sample further to 33 stars by investigating the line signal-to-noise (S/N) ratios as described in Sect.~\ref{h2measurement .}
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, we wished to approach the optical depth problem, and to do this, we needed stars with a literature $A_{\rm V = 0.$, only a few of which have a sufficient S/N in the H$_2$ lines.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We therefore added TW~Hya to our sample.

elements.sent-2411-077: Since some of the stars exhibit \mbox{H$_{2}$} lines with broad wings, which may originate in disk winds (i.e., in a location different from the main emission; \citet{Gangi2023 ), we decided to cut them by fitting the lines with a Gaussian.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We are aware that a Voigt profile resembles the instrument profile better.
elements.sent-2411-079: To account for this, we exemplarily fit all lines of MY~Lup also with a Voigt profile and found fluxes for all lines that were higher by approximately 20\%, which altered our resulting $A_V$ and $R_V$ values for this star only within our errors since the higher flux values cancel out in the ratios.
elements.sent-2411-080: We therefore decided for the Gaussian fit, since it is more stable, and we assumed that the flux measured by this Gaussian originates in the inner disk.
elements.sent-2411-081: To improve the fitting in regions with many lines, we determined the mean background continuum in an adjacent wavelength region for each of our lines and used this as the offset in the fit.
elements.sent-2411-082: To obtain errors for these fitted line fluxes, we applied a bootstrap analysis and varied the spectral flux within its error.
elements.sent-2411-083: We computed the Gaussian flux of the line 1,000 times and took the standard deviation of these computations as the error of the line flux.
elements.sent-2411-084: The typical line flux errors are between 2 and 8\% for many stars; the flux errors of very few lines of these stars lie above 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-085: Nevertheless, the data for a number of stars are noisier.
elements.sent-2411-086: We therefore excluded stars from our analysis whose S/N ratio of the lines was below 10 for more than six lines because the lines then typically become useless.
elements.sent-2411-087: This reduced our sample from 52 to 34 stars.
elements.sent-2411-088: In addition to these purely statistical errors, systematic errors in the line fluxes might be caused by flux variability during the exposure time.
elements.sent-2411-089: From the raw data of TW~Hya, which exhibits the highest observed fluxes of our sample stars, we estimated these errors to be about 5\% or lower, which is about the same as we found for the statistical error.
elements.sent-2411-090: We therefore only applied our statistical errors in our analysis.

elements.sent-2411-100: With only this reddening law, the measured line fluxes from the four progressions [1,7], [1,4], [0,1], and [0,2], their known transition probabilities $A_{ij}$, and the assumption that the lines are optically thin, we used the following method for deriving $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-101: An alternative method is described in Appendix \ref{method1 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: For reference, we call these methods Method I and II where needed for clarity.
elements.sent-2411-103: For each progression, we first computed the theoretically expected flux ratios from their Einstein coefficients A$_{ik}$ and their central wavelength by using \begin{equation}r_{theo}=\frac{F_{ik}}{F_{im}}=\frac{E_{ik}\cdot n_{i} \cdot A_{ik}}{E_{im} \cdot n_{i} \cdot A_{im}}=\frac{\lambda_{im}\cdot A_{ik}}{\lambda_{ik}\cdot A_{im}},\end{equation} where $E_{ik}$ is the level energy, n$_{i}$ is the level population number of the upper level, which is shared by all lines from a single progression, and $\lambda_{ik}$ is the central wavelength of the line.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}r_{theo}=\frac{F_{ik}}{F_{im}}=\frac{E_{ik}\cdot n_{i} \cdot A_{ik}}{E_{im} \cdot n_{i} \cdot A_{im}}=\frac{\lambda_{im}\cdot A_{ik}}{\lambda_{ik}\cdot A_{im}},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: This only holds for optically thin line transitions (see also \citet{Ardila2002 ).}
elements.sent-2411-105: The method was introduced for [S II] lines, which share the same upper level, by \citet{Miller1968 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: We decided to use all permutations of the reliably measured H$_2$, in a similar fashion as \citet{Hartigan2007 , that is, we did not use only line ratios relative to some specific line, for example, the reddest line in each progression.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This resulted in $(n(n-1)\cdot 2)$ pairs, with $n$ being the number of measured line fluxes in the progression (because we did not consider inverse pairs).
elements.sent-2411-108: To obtain an estimate of $A_{V}$, we compared the ratios of our dereddened fluxes to these theoretical ratios.
elements.sent-2411-109: To this end, we calculated dereddened fluxes on an arbitrary grid of $R_{V}$ ranging from 1.5 to 6.2 in steps of 0.08 and of $A_{V}$ ranging from 0.01 to 4.44\,mag in steps of 0.075\,mag.
elements.sent-2411-110: Next, we computed the respective dereddened line ratios and compared them to the theoretical ratios for each $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ combination.
elements.sent-2411-111: Specifically, we determined the best-fitting R$_{\mathrm{V }$ and $A_{V}$ by finding the minimum of the quadratic form \begin{equation}C=\sum_{k=1}^{p} \frac{(r_{meas}-r_{theo})^{2}}{\sigma_{k}^{2}},\end{equation} where $\sigma_{k}$ is the observational uncertainty of the measured and dereddened flux ratio $r_{meas}$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Using a logarithmic form of our $C$ variant, \citet{Hartigan2007 showed $C_{log}$ to have similar statistical properties as $\chi^{2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Errors on the dereddened line ratios, $\sigma_{k}$, were obtained by error propagation.
elements.sent-2411-114: We tested the method by calculating some simulations, that is, we constructed a flux vector for each progression based on the branching ratios and reddened this flux with a known $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We then added Gaussian noise to the flux vector according to different relative errors.
elements.sent-2411-116: We used $A_{V}$=0.8\,mag and $R_{V}$=3.1 and added noise to the simulated fluxes corresponding to a relative error of 0.05.
elements.sent-2411-117: This is expected to represent most of our data well.
elements.sent-2411-118: We also performed test calculations with an error of 0.08 and found similar results.
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth, clip]{graphs/av_test.png} \caption{\label{testsim1} Flux ratios for the individual progressions of H$_{2}$ lines for simulated noisy data with relative flux errors of 0.05. We show the progressions as indicated at the lower or upper right corner of each panel. The line quotient gives the wavelength of the two involved lines for each ratio. They are ordered by increasing wavelength difference between the two lines. In the four upper panels, the black dots show the theoretical flux ratios. The red dots show the flux ratios from the simulated line fluxes. The black lines show the ratios of the dereddened flux (together with the dereddened flux error ratios) for $R_V$=3.1 and $A_V$=0.0,0.5,1.0,1.5,3.0. The red and blue lines denote the same for $R_V$=2.1 and 4.1 for $A_V$=1.0 and 3.0, respectively. We note that some of the lines are almost identical, which again indicating the degeneracy of $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$. Three of the four lower panels show contour plots for an increasingly worse fitting of the $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ values. We show the 68\%, 90\%, and 95\% confidence interval as a black, blue, and yellow line, respectively. The numbers in these lines state the $C\sim\chi^{2}$s for the respective confidence level and are also given in the color bar. The red dot marks the position of the minimum of the distribution, and the black asterisks mark the values chosen for the simulation. In the bottom right panel, we overplot the 160 best-fit $C$s for every progression. The progression [1,7] is represented by cyan dots, progression [1,4] is represented by blue triangles, and progression [0,1] is represented by red asterisks. Our retrieved extinction values are marked as a black dot with error bars. } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-119: We then injected the simulated data in our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-120: The retrieved values are shown in Fig. \ref{testsim1 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: For each progression, a degeneracy of $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ is visible in the lower half of the panels in Fig.~\ref{testsim1 : The lower and higher values of $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ have rather similar transmission curves in the considered wavelength range, as was described by \citet{McJunkin2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: We show in Fig.~\ref{degeneration the transmission curves for three pairs of $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$.}
elements.sent-2411-123: While the shape of the transmission curve is quite similar in the wavelength range covered by UV~H$_2$ lines, which would therefore require data with a high S/N to distinguish between them, the lines at redder wavelength would most probably allow us to break the degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-124: Unfortunately, there are no H$_2$ lines redward of 1600\AA\, with a sufficiently high S/N for our purposes.
elements.sent-2411-125: A possible solution may be CO lines, but it is beyond the scope of this paper to investigate these lines with respect to extinction.
elements.figure-2411-004: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth, clip]{graphs/degeneration_zoom.png} % \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth, clip]{graphs/degeneration.png} \caption{\label{degeneration} Transmission calculated for $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ pairs as given in the legend and normalized at 1600\,\AA, which marks the longest wavelength of the lines we considered. The small vertical bars mark the position of the lines we used. Top: Our considered wavelength range. Bottom: Zoom out to show that lines at longer wavelength would probably allow us to break the degeneracy. } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-126: An interesting feature shown in the upper four panels in Fig.~\ref{testsim1 is the different range of ratios for the different progressions.}
elements.sent-2411-127: This shows that progression [0,2] is the least and progression [0,1] is the most sensitive progression.
elements.sent-2411-128: The four lower panels in Fig.~\ref{testsim1 show that for all progressions, the derived best-fitting values are found within the 68\% confidence level, although the minimum of the distribution is located at different positions.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Progression [0,2] does not work well for the observed stars, but typically shows deviating results.
elements.sent-2411-130: Moreover, for many stars, for example, for the stars DK~Tau~A and SY~Cha, it has only two measured line fluxes and therefore cannot be used for these stars.
elements.sent-2411-131: Moreover, it has generally the lowest range of ratios and should therefore be the least sensitive progression.
elements.sent-2411-132: We therefore omitted it from the further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-133: We do not show the $C$ distribution for this progression here, but replace it by an overlay of the 160 best $C$ values for each of the three other progressions.
elements.sent-2411-134: The injected $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ value is found roughly at the intersection of the three progressions.
elements.sent-2411-135: By combining information from these three progressions, which are by chance influenced differently by the inserted random noise, the degeneracy between $A_V$ and $R_V$ can therefore be broken, and the true value can be retrieved.
elements.sent-2411-136: Each progression may best be described by a different combination of $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-137: To obtain the overall best-fit $A_V$ and $R_V$, the $C\sim\chi^2$ of the different progressions is frequently added to find the best-fit values, but this poses several problems in our case.
elements.sent-2411-138: First, the absolute $C$ values differ from one progression to the next, so that one progression may dominate the result of the addition.
elements.sent-2411-139: Second, some progressions have very steep $C$ valleys for low $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ combinations, which do not align very well between the different progressions.
elements.sent-2411-140: Furthermore, the $C$ contours fan out for combinations of higher $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ values, so that the overall lowest summed $C$ may be found for high $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ values.
elements.sent-2411-141: This probably is an effect of the degeneracy between $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ and is not real.
elements.sent-2411-142: Therefore, we rather searched for a region in which many good fitting models from different progressions are found.
elements.sent-2411-143: To do this, we used the overlap-region of the 160 best-fitting $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$ combinations for each of the progressions.
elements.sent-2411-144: This fixed number of best-fitting models approximately corresponds to the 95\% confidence level for many progressions and stars and limits the influence of progressions that contain many more models within the 95\% confidence region.
elements.sent-2411-145: To identify the position of the largest overlap, we used a box as the search region and counted the number of best-fitting $C$s in this region.
elements.sent-2411-146: Then we shifted the box for half its size along each axis.
elements.sent-2411-147: The central position for which we found most of the best-fitting $C$ values in the box was marked as the overall best fit when at least one $C$ value from each progression was contained in it.
elements.sent-2411-148: When the best-fit $C$ distributions from the different progressions were distinct from each other, the box was enlarged to meet this additional criterion.
elements.sent-2411-149: The extent of the search box can serve as an error estimate.
elements.sent-2411-150: For the simulations, we used an error box of 0.25\,mag for $A_V$ and a box of 0.3 mag for $R_V$.
elements.sent-2411-151: The derived values are $A_V$=0.6\,mag and $R_V$=2.75, which are lower than the injected values, but agree well within the errors.
elements.sent-2411-152: The determination of $A_{V}$ from the UV H$_2$ lines may be hampered by optical depth effects, that is, by self-absorption in the lines: When the levels are thermally populated, this leads to higher populations for the lower vibrational levels.
elements.sent-2411-153: Therefore, the lower vibrational levels have a higher probability of absorbing a fluoresced photon along the line of sight before the photon can escape from the protoplanetary disk.
elements.sent-2411-154: It might be re-emitted out of the line of sight, causing increasing flux loss for increasingly lower levels, which corresponds to lines at increasingly bluer wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-155: This higher opacity for bluer lines mimics the effect of extinction, as was first found by \citet{Wood2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: In a first step, the lines blueward of 1450\,\AA\, are used to determine the self-absorption and a correction for it.
elements.sent-2411-157: In a second step, all lines are then used to determine $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-158: Since $A_{V}$ is not applied to the first step, however, the effect of self-absorption might be overestimated.
elements.sent-2411-159: Even in the case of TW~Hya, where no complication by reddening is expected, the results are ambiguous because \citet{McJunkin2016 found a significant opacity in all lines below 1450\,\AA, while \citet{Herczeg2006 found that progressions [1,4] and [0,1] only have a significant optical depth for lines below 1300 and 1350\,\AA, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Moreover, \citet{Ardila2002 found that the H$_{2}$ levels for different stars with $\nu''\ge$4 are optically thin.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The latter finding would allow us to use all of our lines in the analysis, but following \citet{Herczeg2004 would suggest that we omit the bluest line of the [0,1] progression.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The study by \citet{McJunkin2016 would imply that the two bluest lines of progressions [0,1] and [0,2] are affected by non-negligible line opacity, and that to a smaller extent, even progressions [1,7] and [1.4] may be affected because their bluest lines are located around 1450\,\AA.}
elements.sent-2411-163: To gain further insight without applying full radiative transfer calculations, we estimated the optical depth $\tau_{lu}$ in the lines of the four progressions by using Eqs.
elements.sent-2411-164: We took the lines in the four progressions into account as well as their pumping transitions near Ly$\alpha$, and we neglected all other H$_{2}$ lines.
elements.sent-2411-165: Considering only the pumping lines (since their level population numbers are expected to be much higher than the population numbers of the other transitions) and under the assumption of $\tau=1$, we calculated the total column density $\log(N_{tot )\sim 15.5$ for an assumed excitation temperature $T_{exc =2000$\,K.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: This discrepancy of our high-excitation temperatures leading to rather low column densities can be explained by our omission of all pumping lines blueward of the Ly$\alpha$ line center, which should have even higher population numbers.
elements.sent-2411-167: Moreover, a realistic Voigt profile would lead to higher column densities, as would a $\tau_{lu}$ for the pumping lines higher than one.
elements.sent-2411-168: We therefore consider our $N_{tot}$ value as a lower limit, although it increases (slightly) with temperature.
elements.sent-2411-169: In a second step, we then computed $\tau_{lu}$ in the individual (fluorescent) lines using $N_{tot}$.
elements.sent-2411-170: We list the $\tau_{lu}$ values we estimated under these assumptions in Table~\ref{tab:lines .}
elements.sent-2411-171: As a result of our simplifications, these numbers should be treated with care, but we are positive that we recover the trend, confirming that progression [0,2] is much more affected than progressions [1,7] and [1,4].
elements.sent-2411-172: Since the bluest line of progression [0,2] is most affected, this progression indeed cannot be used because other lines cannot be used due to blends.
elements.sent-2411-173: With these general considerations in mind, we wished to rely only on the measured line flux and refrained from performing full radiative transport calculations to estimate the line opacity effects.
elements.sent-2411-174: Our motivation was to infer the lines that may be influenced by self-absorption from our derived $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ values of the different progressions for the individual stars.
elements.sent-2411-175: From our simulations, we expected to be able to recover the essentially zero extinction of TW~Hya and the other low $A_V$ stars.
elements.sent-2411-176: For the other stars, measuring $R_V$ independently from $A_{V}$ may not lead to significant results given the noise level of the data and the degeneracy between $R_V$ and $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-177: Still, assuming $R_{V}$=3.1, we expected to be able to measure $A_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-178: We list our results for all stars in Table~\ref{tab:results and show our results graphically for a representative selection of stars, like in the following example of TW~Hya in the figures published on Zenodo.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.85\textwidth, clip]{graphs/av_twhya.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \caption{\label{av_twhya} Determination of A$_{\mathrm{V}}$ for TW~Hya. We show the progressions as indicated in the lower or upper right corner of each panel. The top four panels show the measured and dereddened flux ratios (red dots) and the theoretical flux ratios (black dots if the ratio is used for the analysis, gray otherwise). Each black line corresponds to the curve defined by the dereddend measured ratios according to $R_V$=3.1 and $A_V$=0.0,0.5,1.0,1.5,3.0. The red and blue lines denote the same for $R_V$=2.1 and 4.1 for $A_V$=1.0 and 3.0, respectively (the vertical bars are the dereddened errors of the ratios). Three of the four lower panels show the corresponding contour plots of the best-fit models in the $R_{V}$ -- $A_{V}$ plane. We show the 68\%, 90\%, and 95\% confidence interval as a black, blue, and yellow line, respectively. The numbers in these lines state the $C\sim\chi^{2}$s for the respective confidence level and are also given in the color bar. The red dot marks the position of the minimum of the distribution. In the bottom right panel, we overplot the 180 best-fit $C$s for every progression. Progression [1,7] is represented by cyan dots, progression [1,4] is represented by blue triangles, and progression [0,1] is represented by red stars. The black dot with error bars marks the central position and extent of the search box, and it marks the overall best $R_{V}$ -- $A_{V}$ combination. } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-179: We present our results for TW~Hya graphically in Fig.~\ref{av_twhya as an instructive example.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Similar to Fig.~\ref{testsim1 , we show in the upper four panels the measured and the theoretical ratios for each progression.}
elements.sent-2411-181: We realized that all theoretical ratios involving the line at 1442\AA\, from progression [1,7] are below the measured ratios, and therefore, the measured fluxes must be erroneous, because they cannot be reached by any dereddening, as shown by the colored curves.
elements.sent-2411-182: Since the line is near the beginning of an echelle order of the STIS spectrograph, we omitted it from the analysis The same is true for the line at 1547\AA\, from progression [1,4], which is severely blended with \ion{C {iv .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Fig.~\ref{av_twhya shows for progression [1,7] that two additional theoretical values are below the measured values.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Both ratios involve the line (1-7)R(6) at 1500.45\AA, which shows a satisfying fit.
elements.sent-2411-185: Since the deviation is only small and within the 3$\sigma$ error for the measured values, we still used the ratios.
elements.sent-2411-186: For TW~Hya, we find an overall $A_{V}$=0.3$\pm$0.1\,mag and $R_{V}$=1.65$\pm$0.15.
elements.sent-2411-187: We therefore conclude that our analysis for TW~Hya favors a low but nonzero $A_{V}$ in combination with a very steep $R_{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-188: For the other six stars with low $A_{V}$ literature values, we find best-fit values of $A_{V =0.0$\,mag (for a wide variety of formal $R_V$ values for the individual stars).}
elements.sent-2411-189: We show the obtained fits in figures published on Zenodo (see Table~\ref{tab:results ).}
elements.sent-2411-190: The stars with nonzero $A_V$ fall in three categories depending on the number of progressions that can be used to infer $A_V$ (which we therefore list in Table~\ref{tab:results ): In the first category, all three progressions led to about the same result.}
elements.sent-2411-191: In the second category, the [0,1] progression led to deviating results and was omitted because the deviation of this progression might be self-absorption.
elements.sent-2411-192: In the third category, all three progressions led to differing results.
elements.sent-2411-193: In this last category, we found six stars for which the fit did not lead to an overlapping result region, and we were unable to assign an $A_V$ and $R_V$ value to these stars.
elements.sent-2411-194: Nevertheless, we computed a mean $A_V$ value for the case of an assumed $R_V=3.1$.
elements.sent-2411-195: We did this also for all other stars to allow for a better comparison to the literature values.
elements.sent-2411-196: We show our mean $A_V$ values derived for $R_V$=3.1 in a comparison to literature $A_V$ values obtained for the same $R_V$ in Fig.~\ref{comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-197: The Pearson correlation coefficients are $r=0.70$ and $p=3.5\times 10^{-6}$, which indicates a correlation.
elements.sent-2411-198: The correlation is even better when we omit TW~Hya, which would leave us with $r=0.80$ and $p=2.7 \times 10^{-8}$.
elements.sent-2411-199: TW~Hya is special in the sense that it strongly deviates between the best $A_V$ values assigned by the box fit of $0.3\pm0.1$, with $R_V$=1.65 and the best mean $A_V$=1.2 for $R_V$=3.1.
elements.sent-2411-200: This may also slightly influence our progressions [1,7] and [1,4], which would (incorrectly) lead to higher $A_V$ values.
elements.sent-2411-201: This strongly suggests that for TW~Hya, apart from the degeneracy, our results seem to be influenced by self-absorption, which we could not identify for this star because progressions [1,7] and [1.4] may also be influenced.
elements.sent-2411-202: For the other six stars with literature $A_V=0$\,mag, we also find very low to zero $A_V$ values with the box search method, as for a mean $A_V$ at $R_V$=3.1.
elements.sent-2411-203: For three stars, low $R_V$ values are preferred by the box search, which may be caused at least partly by the degeneracy of $A_V$ and $R_V$, since we do not find $R_V<2.0$ for the stars with nonzero $A_V$.
elements.sent-2411-204: Moreover, for five of these seven stars, progression [0,1] leads to significantly higher $A_V$ values.
elements.sent-2411-205: This suggests a contamination by the optical depth for these stars.
elements.sent-2411-206: After a correction for optical depth, they inferred high $R_V$ values for these stars, and in particular, for TW~Hya also a high $A_V=2.0$.
elements.sent-2411-207: We agree with \citet{McJunkin2016 for the high optical depth in DM~Tau, RECX-11, and RECX-15, but were unable to find indications of an optical depth in the H$_{2}$ lines for TW~Hya (which may be affected most, which means that our procedure failed; see above).}
elements.sent-2411-208: Furthermore, our box search method prefers low $R_V$ values, which is in contrast to what \citet{McJunkin2016 found, except for RECX-11, where we find a comparably high $R_V$ value.}
elements.sent-2411-209: For the stars with nonzero $A_V$, we have six stars in common with \citet{McJunkin2016 : AA~Tau, DE~Tau, DR~Tau, HN~Tau, LkCa~15, and UX~Tau.}
elements.sent-2411-210: For the four stars AA~Tau, DE~Tau, HN~Tau, and LkCa~15, the [0,1] progression leads to higher $A_V$ values, but for AA~Tau and HN~Tau, all three progressions differ.
elements.sent-2411-211: This is also the case for DR~Tau, but progression [0,1] does not lead to the highest $A_V$ values and therefore does not indicate any optical depth effects.
elements.sent-2411-212: For UX~Tau, we find the $A_V$ values of progression [0,1] to be only slightly higher than those of the other two progressions.\citet{McJunkin2016 found that optical depth played a role for five of these stars only for a wavelength below $\sim$1300\,\AA, while for UX~Tau, the affected region extended up to 1350\,\AA.}
elements.sent-2411-213: The former would not affect any of our lines, while the latter would affect the bluest line of progression [0,1].
elements.sent-2411-214: It is also curious that the stars for which \citet{McJunkin2016 found the weakest influence of the optical depth are the stars for which in our analysis all three progressions differ.}
elements.sent-2411-215: This discrepancy for the individual stars remains unexplained.
elements.sent-2411-216: Spectra with a higher resolution would help us to distinguish between problems introduced by the method or physical properties, such as H$_{2}$ emission, which partly originates in different places, for example, in the disk wind.
elements.sent-2411-217: Fig.~\ref{comparison shows that our $A_V$ values, for which we used three progressions, are all equal to or higher than the literature values, which can be caused by the unattended self-absorption in the [0,1] progression.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Of the stars for which we used only two progressions, slightly more than half exhibit a lower $A_V$ than the literature value, and we therefore argue that they are usually not influenced by optical depth.
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.50\textwidth, clip]{graphs/comparison.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.50\textwidth, clip]{graphs/comparison_disk.png} \caption{\label{comparison} Comparison of our $A_V$ values for fixed $R_V=3.1$ to the literature values. Top: When all three progressions could be used to infer the mean $A_V$, the stars are represented by red dots, and when two progressions could be used, they are shown with black dots (progression [0,1] omitted). The black line indicates identity. The four stars with literature values of $A_V$=0.0\,mag are offset by 0.02\,mag for clarity of their error bars. Bottom: Same as above, but color-coded with the disk inclination. The dashed gray lines indicate errors of 0.2\,mag.} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-219: An interesting question is whether H$_{2}$ extinction measurements lead to different results than extinction measurements obtained from the optical for some physical reason.
elements.sent-2411-220: This may be expected because we measure extinction toward the origin of the H$_{2}$ emission in the disk, while the measurements obtained from optical data determine extinction toward the star.
elements.sent-2411-221: We therefore show in Fig.~\ref{comparison the extinction values color-coded with disk inclination, where low values indicate face-on disks.}
elements.sent-2411-222: We find a tendency for face-on disks to show higher H$_{2}$ measured than in the literature extinction.
elements.sent-2411-223: This might indicate that the light generated in the hot disk gas must pass the outer rims of the disk, while the light from the star is unaffected by this additional extinction.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, we only have three stars (TW~Hya, DK~Tau~A, both with a face-on disk, and DE~Tau) for which our extinction value is 3$\sigma$ higher than the literature value.
elements.sent-2411-225: We derived $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ values for 34 CTTSs using measurements of fluoresced H$_{2}$ line fluxes.
elements.sent-2411-226: Using simulated data, we showed that it is possible to recover $A_V$ and $R_V$ values for the typical S/N of the UV data using stars with literature values of $A_{V}$ ranging from 0 to 1.6\,mag.
elements.sent-2411-227: Our method used the theoretical line ratios separately for the individual progressions and combined the results from the individual progressions afterward.
elements.sent-2411-228: It allowed us to identify lines that are incompatible with the expected ratio pattern and therefore may be prone to systematic flux measurement errors, for example, by a blend with another line.
elements.sent-2411-229: We computed $C$ values (similar to $\chi^{2}$ values) for the individual progressions on an $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ grid and applied a box search to identify the best-fitting models.
elements.sent-2411-230: This also partly allowed us to break the degeneracy between $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ and to obtain results for the $A_V$ and $R_V$ parameters.
elements.sent-2411-231: In this way, we found low $A_V$ and $R_V$ values for the stars with $A_V$=0.0\,mag in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-232: For TW~Hya and fixing $R_V$=3.1, we found a significant $A_V$=1.2$\pm0.3$\,mag, without any indications of contamination by self-absorption.
elements.sent-2411-233: Although this may indicate that for TW~Hya all our progressions are affected by optical depth effects, it may also indicate additional UV extinction in the vicinity of the star because for most other stars with face-on disks our $A_V$ values are also higher than the literature values.
elements.sent-2411-234: For stars with nonzero $A_V$ literature values, we found lower as well as higher $R_V$ values than the canonical $R_V$=3.1.
elements.sent-2411-235: For a mean $A_V$ obtained from two or three progressions at $R_V$=3.1, we found reasonable agreement with literature values.
elements.sent-2411-236: For the progressions we used, we found that progression [0,2] cannot be used due to a combination of unusable lines and because it is affected by self-absorption.
elements.sent-2411-237: For many stars, the same is true for progression [0,1], which was excluded from the $A_V$ determination when it exhibited systematically steeper $R_V$-$A_V$ distributions than the other progressions.
elements.sent-2411-238: Progressions [1,4] and [1,7] seem to be usable for all stars (except perhaps for TW~Hya).
elements.sent-2411-239: In summary, the exact $A_V$ values that we derived may be affected by some systematic uncertainties such as optical depth effects.
elements.sent-2411-240: However, the values that result from the simple reconstruction are quite close to the literature values and only allow some but not much additional UV extinction in the vicinity of the warm disk, where the H$_{2}$ fluorescent lines are created.
elements.sent-2411-241: We therefore argue that the H$_2$-derived $A_V$ values, and the literature $A_V$ for that matter, are good estimates of the extinction toward the inner warm disk.
elements.sent-2411-242: The general agreement between the literature and our H$_2$-based $A_V$ values suggests that no (significant) additional extinction sources exist between the star and the disk.
elements.sent-2411-243: We found no indication for additional UV extinction either, for instance, by steep extinction laws.
elements.sent-2411-244: This is particularly relevant when modeling the UV spectra of accreting stars because our values provide an estimate of the UV-$A_V$ that cannot be exceeded.
elements.sent-2411-245: Specifically in the context of models that describe the stellar UV spectrum as a combination of accretion columns, it is possible that some of these accretion fluxes can be immediately absorbed in the vicinity of the star, but our results indicate no additional UV-extinction.
elements.sent-2411-246: Therefore, the H$_2$-based or standard $A_V$ values should also be used to redden the calculated UV emission from the accretion columns for comparison with observations.
elements.sent-2411-247: In the future, better (higher S/N) UV H$_2$ line fluxes together with simultaneously treated extinction and optical depth will eventually allow one to derive the extinction toward the inner disk.

elements.section-2411-008: Total flux in a single progression (Method II)
elements.sent-2411-257: Here we discuss an alternative method that is conceptually even simpler than the method introduced above but lacks certain diagnostics, which makes it inferior in the end.
elements.sent-2411-258: This alternative method is based on the fact that photon flux in an individual line, $F^{* _{ij}$, is given by the total flux $F[i,k]$ in progression [i,k] by \begin{equation}F^{*}_{ij} = F[i,k] \cdot B_{ij},\end{equation} where $B_{ij}$ is the accompanying branching ratio, defined as $B_{ij =A_{ij /\sum A_{[i,k]}$ with the spontaneous emission Einstein coefficient $A_{ij}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-259: Since we measure the reddened energy flux $F^{' _{ij}$ this leads to \begin{equation}F^{'}_{ij}=\tau(\lambda;A_{V},R_{V})\cdot F[i,k] \cdot \frac{hc}{\lambda_{ij}}\cdot B_{ij},\end{equation} where $\tau(\lambda;A_{V , R_{V )$ is the transmission for wavelength $\lambda$ due to reddening and $\frac{hc {\lambda_{ij }$ converts photon flux to energy flux.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: For a more detailed discussion we refer to Appendix~\ref{app:method1 .}
elements.sent-2411-261: For any progression [i,k] and $n$ measured line fluxes in that progression, this leads to a set of $n$ equations with the three free parameters: $F[i,k]$, $R_{V}$ and $A_{V}$; the latter two determine $\tau(\lambda,A_{V ,R_{V )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: Therefore, $n\geq3$ lines would be already sufficient to provide an extinction estimate, and more lines can be used to optimize the result using, e.\,g.. the $\chi^{2}$ method.
elements.sent-2411-263: In fact, only three or four H$_2$ lines provide reliable line fluxes in some progressions.
elements.sent-2411-264: The extension from using only one progression to using more progressions is straight forward, since each additional progression introduces only one additional unknown to the set of equations as $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ stay the same.
elements.sent-2411-265: Therefore a large number of progressions with each having several measured line fluxes is desirable.
elements.sent-2411-266: We chose progressions [1,7], [1,4], and [0,1] for this method, since test calculations for our stars show that progression [0,2] only enlarged $\chi^{2}$ without altering the results notably.
elements.sent-2411-267: Moreover, we use an $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ grid for the optimization and fit only the F[a,b] values for each $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$-pair; we then record the $\chi^2$-value for each $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$-pair and determine the best extinction values together with its confidence range from these $\chi^2$-values.
elements.sent-2411-268: To test this approach, we simulated data like for Method I and show the results in Fig.~\ref{testsim for random realizations of the `observed' flux vector.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Figure~\ref{testsim shows that small relative errors usually allow us to recover $A_{V}$, even $R_{V}$ for small or negligible measurement errors.}
elements.sent-2411-270: For the data at hand, a relative error of 0.05 best describes the measurements and allows us to recover $A_{V}$ values from 0.0\,mag to 0.5\,mag, while larger errors introduce subsequently larger uncertainties.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testnoerrav05err01.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testav05err05.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testav05err10.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testav00err05.png} \caption{\label{testsim} Retrieval of $A_{V}$ by $\chi^{2}$ for different $R_{V}$ as marked in the legend for simulated data. Input data is $R_{V}$=3.1 and $A_{V}$=0.5\,mag except for the lower right panel, where $A_{V}$=0.0\,mag Top: Relative error of 0.01, but no statistical noise in the data. Upper middle: Relative error of 0.05, also noise in the data. Lower middle: Relative error of 0.10, also noise in the data. Bottom: Relative error of 0.05, also noise in the data. } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-271: The line emissivity (units: erg\,cm$^{-3}$\,s$^{-1}$) of a transition from an upper level $u = \nu',J'$ to an lower level $l=\nu'',J''$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{EQ_1} j_{ul} = \frac{1}{4\pi} n_u\, A_{ul}\, E_{ul}\,,\end{equation} with $A_{ul}$ being the Einstein coefficient, $n_u$ the number density of the energy level $E_u$, and $E_{ul}$ being the energy difference between the two states.
elements.sent-2411-272: The energy difference for each transition is associated with the release of a photon at wavelength $\lambda_{ul}$ via \begin{equation}\label{EQ_11} E_{ul} = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{ul}} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-273: At this point we introduce the branching ratio for a progression: \begin{equation}\label{EQ_2} B_{ul} = \frac{A_{ul}}{\sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul}}\, ,\end{equation} where the sum goes over all transitions in the progression [$\nu',J'$].
elements.sent-2411-274: For $N$ considered transitions, i.e. number of lines in a progression, we can calculate the following expression using Eqs.\,\ref{EQ_1 and \ref{EQ_2 :\begin{equation}\label{EQ_3} \frac{j_{ul}}{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} = \frac{1}{4\pi} n_u \sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-275: Equation\,\ref{EQ_3 gives the same constant for every transition, i.e. it is independent of the considered transition $u\longrightarrow l$ We can add all these expressions for the $N$ transitions to arrive at\begin{equation}\frac{1}{N}\sum_{[\nu',J']} \frac{j_{ul}}{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} = \frac{1}{4\pi} n_u\,\sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-276: By summing up all emissivities divided by their individual branching ratios and individual photon energy we get a constant that depends only on the sum of all relevant Einstein coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-277: The Method I described here utilises the $N$ equations (Eq. \ref{EQ_3 ) in the following way: The extinction law with parameters $A_V$ and $R_V$ is a wavelength dependent function, here introduced as $\tau(\lambda; A_V, R_V)$, which reduces effectively the intrinsic line emissivities $j_{ul ^{\text{int }$ to the observed line emissivities $j_{ul ^{\text{obs }$ via \begin{equation}j_{ul}^{\text{obs}} =\tau(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{ul}^{\text{int}} \,.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-278: Introducing the extinction into Eq.~\ref{EQ_3 leads to a new set of $N$ equations, which are now \begin{equation}\label{EQ_4} \frac{1}{4\pi} n_u \sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} = \frac{ j_{ul}^{\text{int}}}{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} = \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{ul}^{\text{obs}} }{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{EQ_4} \frac{1}{4\pi} n_u \sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} = \frac{ j_{ul}^{int}}{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} = \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{ul}^{obs} }{B_{ul}\,E_{ul}} .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-279: Incorporating Eq.\,\ref{EQ_11 into Eq.\,\ref{EQ_4 leads to \begin{equation}\label{EQ_5} \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{ul}^{\text{obs}}\,\cdot \lambda_{ul} }{B_{ul} } = 1/4\pi hc\,\cdot\, n_u \sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} = \text{const} .\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{EQ_5} \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{ul}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{ul} }{B_{ul} } = 1/4\pi hc\,\cdot\, n_u \sum_{[\nu',J']} A_{ul} = const .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-280: From observations we can measure the $N$ line fluxes and therefore line emissivities (see the next section) and get a set of $N$ equations with three parameters $A_V, R_V,$ and the constant.
elements.sent-2411-281: In the following we give an example with 4 measured lines, where $j_{51 , j_{52 , j_{53 , j_{54}$ are the measured line emissivities, $A_{51 , A_{52 , A_{53 , A_{54}$ are the Einstein coefficients, and $\lambda_{51 , \lambda_{52 , \lambda_{53 , \lambda_{54}$ are the photon wavelengths.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-282: The set of $N=4$ equations are \begin{equation}\frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{51}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{51}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{51} }{B_{51} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{52}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{52}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{52} }{B_{52} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{53}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{53}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{53} }{B_{53} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{54}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{54}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{54} }{B_{54} } = C.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{51}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{51}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{51} }{B_{51} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{52}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{52}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{52} }{B_{52} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{53}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{53}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{53} }{B_{53} } = C \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{54}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, j_{54}^{obs}\,\cdot \lambda_{54} }{B_{54} } = C.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-283: If one observes for an individual star a second progression, the constant $C$ changes, but $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ stay the same, so that just one additional variable is added and another $N'$ equations, depending on the number $N'$ of observed lines in the second progression.
elements.sent-2411-284: In Method II we use line fluxes and not emissivities.
elements.sent-2411-285: Here we show, how one gets from the line emissivities $j_{ul}$ (units: erg\,cm$^{-3}$\,s$^{-1}$) to the integrated line fluxes $F_{ul}$ (units: erg\,cm$^{-2}$\,s$^{-1}$): The emitting gas is confined to a volume $V_{\text{emit }$ located at the distance $D$ to the observer.}
elements.sent-2411-286: The total energy per unit time in an emission line $u\longrightarrow l$ radiated from the emitting gas is then \begin{equation}L_{ul} = \int_{V_{\text{emit}}}\,j_{ul}\,\text{d}V .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-287: This is the line luminosity (units: erg\,s$^{-1}$).
elements.sent-2411-288: The integrated line flux is then given by \begin{equation}F_{ul} = \frac{L_{ul}}{4\pi D^2} = \frac{\int_{V_{\text{emit}}}\,j_{ul}\,\text{d}V}{4\pi D^2} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-289: If all emission lines emerge from the same volume at the same distance we have \begin{equation}F_{ul} = \frac{\int_{V_{\text{emit}}} \text{d}V}{4\pi D^2}\,\cdot \,j_{ul} \quad \longrightarrow \quad F_{ul} = \text{const} \,\cdot \,j_{ul} .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-290: Therefore, we can substitute the line emissivities with the line fluxes,\begin{equation}\label{EQ_6} \frac{ \tau^{-1}(\lambda_{ul}; A_V, R_V)\,\cdot\, F_{ul}^{\text{obs}}\,\cdot \lambda_{ul} }{B_{ul} } = \text{const} ,\end{equation} holds, though with a different constant.
elements.sent-2411-291: Since for no star a distinct $\chi^{2}$ minimum can be identified we stick to a fixed $R_{V}$=3.1.
elements.sent-2411-292: We estimate the $1\sigma$ variance in $A_{V}$ by using $\chi^{2 +1$.}
elements.sent-2411-293: For TW~Hya we do not get $A_{V}$=0\,mag, instead for $R_{V}$=3.1 we measure an $A_{V =1.3\pm 0.5$\,mag, which should be clearly too high for this star, though also Method I leads to a similar value, if $R_{V}$=3.1 is chosen.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Our overall lowest $\chi^{2}$ measurement corresponds to $A_{V =0.5\pm0.2$\,mag and $R_{V}$=2.1, which indicates a very steep extinction law but is in agreement with our Method I.}
elements.sent-2411-295: The low $R_{V}$ value may be caused by the \mbox{H$_{2}$} lines at wavelength lower than about 1450\AA\, not being optically thin any more.
elements.sent-2411-296: For SY~Cha we also obtain $A_{V =1.3\pm 0.75$\,mag for a fixed $R_{V}$=3.1, which is in agreement with Method I.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Fixing R$_{V}$ to the preferred value of Method I, to 2.1, would lead to identical values of $A_{V}$=0.5\,mag for Method I and Method II.
elements.sent-2411-298: For DK~Tau~A we derive $A_{V =1.4\pm 0.9$\,mag, which is in agreement with Method I.}
elements.sent-2411-299: For MY~Lup we derive $A_{V =1.1\pm 0.7$\,mag, which is lower than the value obtained with Method I.}
elements.sent-2411-300: Nevertheless, Method I yields consistent results, if we there also fix $R_{V}$=3.1.
elements.sent-2411-301: Unfortunately, Method II leads generally to large error bars that prevent us from identifying unique values for $A_{V}$ for the individual stars.
elements.sent-2411-302: However, the error ranges for all stars except TW~Hya overlap if one allows for the low $R_{V}$ value.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testtwhya1.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testsycha1.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testdktau1.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth, clip]{graphs/testmylup1.png} %\vspa4ce{-0.4mm} \caption{\label{methodi} Estimation of $A_{V}$ by $\chi^{2}$ for different $R_{V}$ as marked in the legend. Top: For TW~Hya. Upper middle: For SY~Cha. Lower middle: For DK~Tau. Bottom: For MY~Lup. } \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-303: Method I has several advantages over Method II.
elements.sent-2411-304: First, while we treat all progressions together in Method II, for Method I we treat each progression separately and combine these then into one result for the A$_{V}$ of each star.
elements.sent-2411-305: Though this merging of the results comes with difficulties, it allows progressions with differing contributions to be identified.
elements.sent-2411-306: Second, if theoretical ratios lower than the observed ratios are found, the pattern in the quotients affected often allows to identify the line, which may contribute an erroneous flux.
elements.sent-2411-307: Since only some of these lines show also unacceptable Gaussian fits, this allows for some additional 'hand-picking' of the used lines.
elements.sent-2411-308: We think that the better usage of the information content of the measurements together with the better control of error introducing drawbacks of Method I is allowing us to break the degeneracy of $A_{V}$ and $R_{V}$ (at least partly) by comparing the different progressions.
